title: Type Inference
description: Let Kotlin figure out the data types for you.

slides.title: Smart Language 🧠
slides.content:
## Kotlin is Clever

In the previous lesson, you saw us write `val score: Int = 100`.

But wait... `100` is obviously an integer. Does Kotlin really need us to specifically write `: Int`? 

The answer is **No**!

slides.title: Type Inference 🕵️
slides.content: Kotlin has a feature called **Type Inference**. If you give a variable an initial value immediately, Kotlin can guess its type automatically by looking at the data.

quizData.question: If you write `val height = 1.85`, what data type will Kotlin infer for `height`?
quizData.explanation: Because 1.85 has a decimal point, Kotlin automatically infers that it is a Double.

slides.title: When is it required? ⚠️
slides.content:
## Type Inference Limits

You only need to explicitly write the type if you **do not** give the variable a value immediately upon creation.
Even though Kotlin is smart, it is also strictly typed. Once a variable's type is decided (either inferred or stated), it can **never** hold a different type of data.

quizData.question: Can you store the `String` "Hello" in a variable that was previously inferred as an `Int`?

options.text: Yes, it will automatically change its type
No, Kotlin variables cannot change their data type once set
Yes, but you get a warning

quizData.explanation: Kotlin is statically typed. An integer variable remains an integer forever, even if it's a `var`.
